function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing discloses two distinct voting mechanisms: (i) Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and (ii) combined Class A and Class B voting power determining the remaining board seats and general shareholder matters. Explain precisely how this dual-mechanism structure differs economically and practically from a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure in which all directors are elected by the combined class vote. What is the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest?

I need to carefully examine the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the exact dual-class voting mechanism and governance structure. Let me start by parsing the filing and then retrieving the relevant governance disclosures.
steps.reasoning_content: This is a detailed governance and structural question about SpaceX's dual-class voting mechanism. Let me first parse the S-1 filing to understand the exact voting structure described, then compare it to a conventional 10:1 dual-class structure.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spaceX_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing is loaded. Now let me retrieve the voting structure and governance details.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the complete description of Class A and Class B share voting rights, voting power per share, and how directors are elected. Include the dual-mechanism structure where Class B shareholders vote as a class to elect a majority of directors, and the combined Class A and Class B voting power for remaining board seats and general shareholder matters. From {{spaceX_s1}}

Retrieve the description of the board of directors composition, the total number of directors, and the split between directors elected by Class B shareholders as a class versus directors elected by combined voting power. From {{spaceX_s1}}

results.content:
# SpaceX Dual-Class Voting Structure: Complete Description

## Voting Rights Per Share

| Share Class | Votes Per Share | Voting Status |
|------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Class A Common Stock** | **1 vote** per share | Full voting rights |
| **Class B Common Stock** | **10 votes** per share | Full voting rights (superior) |
| **Class C Common Stock** | **No voting rights** | Except as required by law |

---

## Director Election: The Dual Mechanism

The charter establishes a **split board election system**:

### Mechanism 1 — Class B Shareholders Elect 51% of Directors
> *"Holders of our Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors (rounded up to the nearest whole number)** for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding."*

These directors are called **"Class B Directors."**

- **Removal:** Class B Directors may be removed with or without cause only by the affirmative vote of holders of at least a **majority of the voting power of outstanding Class B shares**, voting separately as a class.
- **Vacancies:** Filled by the affirmative vote of Class B holders (majority of voting power, voting separately) **or** by the remaining Class B Directors — and **not by any other persons**.

**Practical example:** With an 8-member board, Class B shareholders elect **5 directors** (51% of 8 = 4.08, rounded up to 5).

### Mechanism 2 — Combined Voting Elects Remaining Directors
> *"Holders of all classes of our voting common stock, voting together as a single class, are entitled to elect the remaining directors (the 'Common Stock Directors')."*

These remaining directors are called **"Common Stock Directors"** and are elected by **all shareholders voting together** — Class A and Class B combined.

**Initial board composition (8 directors):**

| Class B Directors (elected by Class B alone) | Common Stock Directors (elected by combined vote) |
|---|---|
| Elon Musk | Ira Ehrenpreis |
| Gwynne Shotwell | Randy Glein |
| Antonio J. Gracias | Steve Jurvetson |
| Donald Harrison | |
| Luke Nosek | |

---

## General Shareholder Matters: Combined Voting

> *"Class A shareholders and Class B shareholders will vote together as a single class on all matters to be voted on by shareholders under our charter."*

For **all matters other than Class B Director elections**, Class A and Class B vote together as a single class, with each share carrying its respective vote weight (1 vote for Class A, 10 votes for Class B).

---

## Additional Class B Class Voting Rights (Voting Separately as a Class)

Beyond director elections, Class B holders voting separately as a class also control:

1. **Removal of Elon Musk** from his board and leadership roles (CEO and Chairman) — requires approval of at least a majority of the voting power of outstanding Class B shares
2. **Charter amendments** affecting Class B rights, powers, preferences, or privileges (including Class B Director provisions)
3. **Certain mergers, combinations, or sales** as specified in the charter

---

## Voting Power Concentration

| Metric | Pre-Offering (Musk) | Post-Offering (Musk) |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Class A shares | 849,494,440 (12.3%) | — |
| Class B shares | 5,569,053,075 (93.6%) | — |
| **Combined voting power** | **85.1%** | ~% (not yet disclosed) |

As the document states:

> *"Mr. Musk will beneficially own a majority of the outstanding shares of our Class B common stock and a majority of the voting power of the common stock (the Class A common stock and the Class B common stock voting together) and therefore **will be able to elect all the members of our board**."*

This means Musk controls:
- **All 5 Class B Directors** through his majority ownership of Class B stock (voting as a separate class)
- **All 3 Common Stock Directors** through his majority of combined voting power (Class A + Class B voting together)

---

## Conversion Mechanics

- **Voluntary:** Each Class B share is convertible at any time at the holder's option into one share of Class A
- **Automatic:** Each Class B share converts automatically into one share of Class A upon any **Transfer** (whether or not for value), except for **Permitted Transfers** (inter-family transfers and transfers to certain qualifying entities)
- **Effect of conversion:** Over time, conversion of Class B to Class A increases the relative voting power of those Class B holders who retain their shares

---

## Key Takeaway

The structure creates a **permanent dual-mechanism** where Class B shareholders — effectively Elon Musk — control a guaranteed **majority of the board** through their separate class vote, while also controlling all remaining board seats and every general corporate matter through their dominant combined voting power derived from the **10:1 vote premium**. This concentration of power exists *"for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding"* and cannot be diluted by public Class A shareholders.

Based on the provided text from the company's prospectus, here is the information regarding the board of directors:

### Total Number of Directors
Upon consummation of the offering, the board will consist of **eight directors**.

### Split Between Director Classes
The directors are split according to the company's dual-class voting structure:
*   **Directors Elected by Class B Shareholders (as a class):** **Five directors**.
    *   *Rule:* The charter provides that holders of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, have the right to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors, rounded up to the nearest whole number**. For a board of eight, 51% (4.12) rounds up to five directors. These are called **"Class B Directors."**
*   **Directors Elected by Combined Voting Power:** **Three directors**.
    *   *Rule:* Holders of Class A and Class B common stock, voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors. These are called **"Common Stock Directors."**

### Initial Board Composition (as expected post-offering)
*   **Class B Directors (elected by Class B shareholders):**
    1.  Elon Musk
    2.  Gwynne Shotwell
    3.  Antonio J. Gracias
    4.  Donald Harrison
    5.  Luke Nosek
*   **Common Stock Directors (elected by combined Class A and Class B voting power):**
    1.  Ira Ehrenpreis
    2.  Randy Glein
    3.  Steve Jurvetson

**Summary:** The board has eight members. Five (a majority) are elected exclusively by Class B shareholders, and three are elected by the combined vote of all common stockholders.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a thorough understanding of the dual-mechanism voting structure. Let me also retrieve information about the conversion mechanics and any other relevant governance details to make my analysis complete.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the details about Class B automatic conversion triggers, permitted transfers, and the anti-dilution effect on Class A shareholders' voting power over time. Also retrieve any disclosure about what happens if all Class B shares convert to Class A. From {{spaceX_s1}}
Retrieve the total number of Class A shares and Class B shares outstanding after the offering, and the total voting power percentage held by Elon Musk or the Musk family. From {{spaceX_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the prospectus, here are the details regarding Class B common stock conversion triggers, permitted transfers, and the effects on voting power.

### **1. Automatic Conversion Triggers for Class B Shares**
Class B shares convert automatically into Class A shares (1-for-1) upon any **"Transfer"** of the share, unless it qualifies as a **"Permitted Transfer."**

*   **Definition of a "Transfer":** This is broadly defined and includes "any sale, assignment, encumbrance, transfer, conveyance, hypothecation, pledge, gift, or other transfer or disposition of any kind of such share of Class B common stock or any legal or beneficial interest in such share, whether or not for value and whether voluntary or involuntary or by operation of law." It also includes transferring voting control via proxy or otherwise.
*   **Voluntary Conversion:** In addition to the automatic trigger, holders can also convert their Class B shares to Class A shares at any time at their option.

### **2. Permitted Transfers (Exceptions to Automatic Conversion)**
Certain transfers, known as "Permitted Transfers," do **not** trigger the automatic conversion to Class A stock. These include transfers to or from:
*   The registered holder of the Class B shares.
*   Certain natural persons who transferred shares or equity awards to "permitted entities" as defined in the charter.
*   Family members of the shareholders specified above.
*   Certain trusts, partnerships, LLCs, corporations, or other entities owned by qualified shareholders, including certain permitted non-profits.
*   Bona fide charitable trusts, certain retirement accounts, and for estate or succession planning purposes.

Transferees who receive shares in a Permitted Transfer become "Permitted Transferees."

### **3. Anti-Dilution Effect on Class A Shareholders' Voting Power**
The conversion mechanism is designed to consolidate voting power over time:
*   As Class B shares (which carry **10 votes per share**) are transferred and converted into Class A shares (which carry **1 vote per share**), the total number of Class B shares decreases.
*   Consequently, **the relative voting power of the remaining Class B shareholders increases** compared to the growing pool of Class A shareholders.
*   The prospectus states: *"The conversion of Class B common stock to Class A common stock will have the effect, over time, of increasing the relative voting power of those holders of Class B common stock who retain their shares."*

### **4. What Happens if All Class B Shares Convert to Class A**
If all Class B shares are converted to Class A shares (either voluntarily or through non-permitted transfers), the **dual-class voting structure would effectively cease**.
*   **Single Class:** All outstanding common stock would be Class A, with each share entitled to **one vote**.
*   **Loss of Special Class B Rights:** The special rights of Class B shareholders to elect a majority of the board and to have a separate class vote on certain charter amendments would **no longer apply**, as there would be no Class B shares outstanding.
*   **Concentrated Control May Persist:** While the *class-based* control ends, a shareholder who retained a large number of converted shares (now Class A) would still hold a significant portion of the company's total voting power. The risk noted in the prospectus is that if a dominant holder (like Elon Musk) were to sell their Class B shares, another Class B holder could gain control before the shares convert.

### **Additional Key Details from the Text:**
*   **Voting Rights:** Class A (1 vote per share), Class B (10 votes per share). Except for specific board election and certain charter amendment votes, they vote together as a single class.
*   **Board Control:** Holders of Class B common stock, voting as a separate class, are entitled to elect **51% of the board of directors**. Removing the CEO/Chairman (Elon Musk) requires a separate majority vote of Class B shareholders.
*   **Issuance Restrictions:** New shares of Class B common stock may only be issued to Elon Musk, his family members, and certain permitted entities.
*   **Preferred Stock Trigger:** The SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock automatically converts into common stock upon a "Qualified IPO" (defined as a public offering with specific market cap and proceeds thresholds) or by holder consent.

Based on the provided text excerpts, the exact total number of Class A and Class B shares outstanding **after the offering** and the specific post-offering voting power percentage for Elon Musk are **not explicitly stated**. The documents contain placeholder text (e.g., "shares" or "%") where these final figures would typically be inserted before publication.

However, we can extract the following related information from the documents:

**1. Pre-Offerring Shares Beneficially Owned by Elon Musk (as of May 1, 2026):**
*   **Class A Common Stock:** 849,494,440 shares
*   **Class B Common Stock:** 5,569,053,075 shares

**2. Key Context on Post-Offerring Voting Power:**
*   The documents consistently state that after the offering, Elon Musk will control a **majority of the voting power** (over 50%).
*   One specific, pre-offering figure is provided: Musk's combined voting power was **85.1%** before the offering.
*   The structure gives Class B shares 10 votes per share and Class A shares 1 vote per share. Musk's holdings are heavily weighted toward Class B shares, ensuring his controlling position.

**3. Foundational Share Count (Pre-Offering Basis):**
*   **Class A Common Stock Outstanding (Pre-Offering):** 6,932,508,000 shares
*   **Class B Common Stock Outstanding (Pre-Offering):** 5,602,790,410 shares

**Conclusion:**
To retrieve the precise **post-offering totals** and Musk's **exact voting power percentage**, one would need to consult the final, published version of the prospectus, where the placeholder blanks would be filled with the definitive numbers from the completed offering. Based on the provided text, the company is structured to ensure Musk maintains majority voting control post-offering, but the final calculated figures are not present in these excerpts.

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed for a comprehensive analysis. Let me calculate some specific scenarios to illustrate the differences.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive analysis comparing SpaceX's dual-mechanism voting structure to a conventional 10:1 dual-class structure.

Key distinctions to draw:

1. **Conventional 10:1 dual-class structure:** All directors are elected by combined vote. Class B's 10:1 vote premium alone must be sufficient to control the board.

2. **SpaceX's dual-mechanism:** Two separate mechanisms:
   - Mechanism 1: Class B shareholders vote SEPARATELY AS A CLASS to elect 51% of directors (majority)
   - Mechanism 2: All shareholders vote TOGETHER to elect remaining directors

The economic and practical differences are profound:

**In a conventional 10:1 structure:**
- If Class A shareholders collectively held, say, 60% of economic interest but Class B holders had enough 10:1 votes to outnumber them, Class B controls the entire board
- BUT: if Class A shareholders collectively held, say, 95% of shares and Class B only 5%, then even with 10:1 votes, Class B would have (5% × 10) / (5% × 10 + 95% × 1) = 50/145 = 34.5% voting power - they would LOSE control
- The 10:1 premium alone is mathematically sufficient only if Class B holds enough economic interest

**In SpaceX's dual-mechanism:**
- Even if Class A shareholders collectively held 99.99% of all shares (economic interest), Class B shareholders STILL elect 51% of the board through their separate class vote
- This is because Mechanism 1 is a CLASS VOTE - it doesn't depend on voting power proportion at all
- Class B holders voting as a class simply need a majority of Class B shares to elect their directors
- This is a STRUCTURAL GUARANTEE that no amount of economic dilution can overcome (as long as any Class B shares remain outstanding)

The specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition:

1. **At most 49% of board seats are contestable** - even if Class A holders have 100% of economic interest
2. **Class A shareholders can ONLY influence the "Common Stock Director" seats** (3 out of 8 in the initial board)
3. **Even for Common Stock Director seats**, Class B's 10:1 vote premium means Class A shareholders need an overwhelming economic majority to outvote Class B
4. **5 out of 8 board seats are permanently beyond Class A shareholders' reach** as long as any Class B shares exist

This is fundamentally different from a conventional dual-class because:
- In a conventional dual-class, if economic dilution of the controlling class reaches a tipping point, the 10:1 premium becomes insufficient to control ALL seats
- In SpaceX's structure, 51% of seats are STRUCTURALLY RESERVED for Class B regardless of economic ownership
- The 10:1 premium only matters for the remaining ~49% of seats and general matters

Let me now write up the comprehensive answer.
Good. Let me also calculate some hypothetical scenarios to illustrate the differences between the two structures.
Let me also calculate a scenario where Class A holds 95% of shares to illustrate the conventional vs. SpaceX difference.

steps.message: Now let me compile the complete analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
Good. In a conventional dual-class structure, if Class B only holds 5% of shares, even with 10:1 votes they'd only have ~34.5% voting power - not enough to control the board. But in SpaceX's structure, they'd still elect 51% of directors through the separate class vote.

Let me now write the comprehensive answer.

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX's Dual-Mechanism Voting Structure vs. a Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class Structure

### I. The Two Voting Mechanisms in the SpaceX Charter

The SpaceX charter establishes two distinct and independent voting mechanisms:

**Mechanism 1 — Class B Separate Class Vote for 51% of Directors:**
- Class B shareholders, voting *separately as a class*, elect 51% of the total authorized directors (rounded up to the nearest whole number).
- On the initial 8-member board, this means Class B holders elect **5 directors** (51% of 8 = 4.08, rounded up to 5). These are designated "Class B Directors."
- This right persists "for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding."
- Removal of Class B Directors requires a majority vote of Class B shareholders *voting as a separate class*—Class A shareholders have no voice whatsoever in this mechanism.
- Vacancies among Class B Directors are filled exclusively by Class B shareholder vote or by remaining Class B Directors.

**Mechanism 2 — Combined Class Vote for Remaining Directors and All Other Matters:**
- All voting common stock (Class A at 1 vote/share; Class B at 10 votes/share) votes *together as a single class* to elect the remaining directors ("Common Stock Directors") and to decide all other shareholder matters.
- On the initial 8-member board, this covers **3 directors** (37.5% of seats).
- The 10:1 vote premium gives Class B holders dominant influence in this combined vote.

---

### II. How This Differs from a Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class Structure

In a **conventional** dual-class structure (e.g., typical Google/Meta-type arrangements), there is a single election mechanism: *all* directors are elected by the combined vote of all shares, with Class B receiving 10 votes per share and Class A receiving 1 vote per share. Control derives entirely from the aggregate voting-power arithmetic.

SpaceX's structure differs in three critical respects:

#### A. A Structural Floor vs. a Mathematical Premium

| Feature | Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class | SpaceX Dual-Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **How control is maintained** | Entirely through arithmetic: the 10:1 vote premium must mathematically produce a majority of total voting power | Two layers: (1) a **structural guarantee** of 51% of board seats via the separate class vote, regardless of economics; (2) the 10:1 vote premium for remaining seats and general matters |
| **What happens if controlling class's economic interest erodes** | If Class B's share count declines (through dilution or conversion) such that their 10× votes fall below 50% of total voting power, they lose control of *all* directors | Class B holders **always** elect 51% of the board as long as *any* Class B shares exist, even if they represent a trivial fraction of total economic interest |
| **Vulnerability to dilution** | The 10:1 premium is mathematically erodable; sufficient new Class A issuance can tip combined voting power | The separate class vote is **immune to dilution**; it is a binary structural right, not a function of share proportions |

**Illustrative example:** Suppose Class B shareholders hold only 5% of total shares outstanding, with Class A holding 95%. In a conventional 10:1 structure, Class B's combined voting power would be:
- (5 × 10) / (5 × 10 + 95 × 1) = 50/145 = **34.5%** — Class B loses board control entirely.

Under SpaceX's structure, Class B would **still elect 51% of the board** (5 of 8 directors) through the separate class vote, despite holding only 34.5% of combined voting power and just 5% of economic interest.

#### B. The Contested-Director Ceiling Is Hard-Capped at 49%

In a conventional dual-class structure, if Class A shareholders collectively acquire enough economic interest to overcome the 10:1 premium in combined voting, they can theoretically elect *all* directors.

Under SpaceX's charter, **Class A shareholders can never elect more than 49% of directors** (rounded down), regardless of how many shares they hold. With an 8-member board, that means at most 3 seats. The remaining 5 are structurally reserved for Class B.

This is not a practical disadvantage that can be overcome with capital—it is a **charter-level structural constraint** that persists until the last Class B share converts.

#### C. The 10:1 Premium Operates Only Within a Constrained Arena

In a conventional structure, the 10:1 premium operates across 100% of board seats and 100% of general matters. In SpaceX's structure, the 10:1 premium only determines outcomes for:
- The ~49% of board seats elected by combined vote (3 of 8 initially)
- All general shareholder matters (mergers, charter amendments not affecting Class B, etc.)

For the ~51% of board seats, the 10:1 premium is irrelevant—those seats are determined solely by the separate class vote.

---

### III. Specific Implication for a Class A Shareholder's Ability to Influence Board Composition

Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a **majority of economic interest** (say, 60%, 70%, or even 95% of shares outstanding), their ability to influence board composition is constrained in the following ways:

**1. Permanent exclusion from 51% of board seats:**
Class A shareholders have **zero** ability to nominate, elect, vote for, or remove Class B Directors. With the initial 8-member board, that means 5 of 8 seats (62.5%) are entirely beyond Class A's reach. This right is not a function of share ownership—it is a structural charter entitlement reserved exclusively for Class B holders.

**2. Severely constrained influence over the remaining ~49% of seats:**
For Common Stock Director seats (3 of 8 initially), Class A and Class B vote together. But Class B shares carry 10 votes each. Even if Class A shareholders held, hypothetically, 91% of shares (vs. 9% Class B), Class B's combined voting power would be:
- (9 × 10) / (9 × 10 + 91 × 1) = 90/181 = **49.7%**

Class A, despite holding 91% of economic interest, would hold barely 50.3% of combined voting power—enough for a razor-thin majority on just 3 seats. At any Class B economic interest above ~9.1%, the 10:1 premium alone gives Class B majority combined voting power, meaning Class B controls *all* seats.

**3. In practice, with disclosed ownership levels, Class A has effectively no influence:**
Pre-offering, Elon Musk held 5,569,053,075 Class B shares (carrying ~55.7 billion votes) and 849,494,440 Class A shares, representing ~88.9% of combined voting power. At these levels, Class B holders control both the separate-class vote (5 seats) and the combined vote (3 seats), giving them control of the **entire** board through both mechanisms simultaneously.

**4. No cumulative or proxy access relief:**
The filing does not disclose cumulative voting, proxy access, or any other mechanism that would allow minority Class A shareholders to concentrate votes on a subset of candidates. For the 3 Common Stock Director seats, Class B's vote weight in the combined election will typically determine the outcome.

---

### IV. Summary Comparison Table

| Dimension | Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class | SpaceX Dual-Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Director election method** | All directors elected by combined vote with 10:1 premium | 51% by Class B separate class vote; ~49% by combined vote |
| **Controlling class needs to hold** | Enough shares so that (shares × 10) > 50% of total voting power | Any non-zero number of Class B shares for 51% of board; voting-power majority for remaining seats |
| **Can economic majority by Class A overcome control?** | Yes, if Class A's share count overwhelms the 10:1 premium | **No**—51% of board is structurally locked for Class B regardless of economics |
| **Maximum board seats Class A can influence** | 100% (if they achieve voting-power majority) | ~49% (hard ceiling) |
| **Vulnerability to dilution** | Mathematically erodable | Class B Director seats are dilution-proof |
| **Persistence** | Ends when 10:1 premium is insufficient | Ends only when the last Class B share converts |

### V. Bottom Line

SpaceX's dual-mechanism structure is **strictly more protective of founder control** than a conventional 10:1 dual-class arrangement. The conventional structure relies solely on arithmetic—a premium that can, in theory, be diluted away. SpaceX's structure layers a **non-arithmetic, structural guarantee** (the separate class vote for 51% of directors) on top of the arithmetic premium. The practical implication is that a Class A shareholder—even one representing a consortium holding a majority of economic interest—cannot influence the election of a majority of the board and faces prohibitive disadvantages in contesting even the minority of seats technically open to combined voting. The Class B separate-class veto is absolute and binary: it exists or it does not, and as long as any Class B shares remain outstanding, it does.

---

**Sources:**
1. SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm — "Description of Capital Stock" section; "Management — Directors and Executive Officers" section; "Security Ownership of Certain Beneficial Owners and Management" section.

## SpaceX's Dual-Mechanism Voting Structure vs. a Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class Structure

### I. The Two Voting Mechanisms in the SpaceX Charter

The SpaceX charter establishes two distinct and independent voting mechanisms:

**Mechanism 1 — Class B Separate Class Vote for 51% of Directors:**
- Class B shareholders, voting *separately as a class*, elect 51% of the total authorized directors (rounded up to the nearest whole number).
- On the initial 8-member board, this means Class B holders elect **5 directors** (51% of 8 = 4.08, rounded up to 5). These are designated "Class B Directors."
- This right persists "for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding."
- Removal of Class B Directors requires a majority vote of Class B shareholders *voting as a separate class*—Class A shareholders have no voice whatsoever in this mechanism.
- Vacancies among Class B Directors are filled exclusively by Class B shareholder vote or by remaining Class B Directors.

**Mechanism 2 — Combined Class Vote for Remaining Directors and All Other Matters:**
- All voting common stock (Class A at 1 vote/share; Class B at 10 votes/share) votes *together as a single class* to elect the remaining directors ("Common Stock Directors") and to decide all other shareholder matters.
- On the initial 8-member board, this covers **3 directors** (37.5% of seats).
- The 10:1 vote premium gives Class B holders dominant influence in this combined vote.

---

### II. How This Differs from a Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class Structure

In a **conventional** dual-class structure (e.g., typical Google/Meta-type arrangements), there is a single election mechanism: *all* directors are elected by the combined vote of all shares, with Class B receiving 10 votes per share and Class A receiving 1 vote per share. Control derives entirely from the aggregate voting-power arithmetic.

SpaceX's structure differs in three critical respects:

#### A. A Structural Floor vs. a Mathematical Premium

| Feature | Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class | SpaceX Dual-Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **How control is maintained** | Entirely through arithmetic: the 10:1 vote premium must mathematically produce a majority of total voting power | Two layers: (1) a **structural guarantee** of 51% of board seats via the separate class vote, regardless of economics; (2) the 10:1 vote premium for remaining seats and general matters |
| **What happens if controlling class's economic interest erodes** | If Class B's share count declines (through dilution or conversion) such that their 10× votes fall below 50% of total voting power, they lose control of *all* directors | Class B holders **always** elect 51% of the board as long as *any* Class B shares exist, even if they represent a trivial fraction of total economic interest |
| **Vulnerability to dilution** | The 10:1 premium is mathematically erodable; sufficient new Class A issuance can tip combined voting power | The separate class vote is **immune to dilution**; it is a binary structural right, not a function of share proportions |

**Illustrative example:** Suppose Class B shareholders hold only 5% of total shares outstanding, with Class A holding 95%. In a conventional 10:1 structure, Class B's combined voting power would be:
- (5 × 10) / (5 × 10 + 95 × 1) = 50/145 = **34.5%** — Class B loses board control entirely.

Under SpaceX's structure, Class B would **still elect 51% of the board** (5 of 8 directors) through the separate class vote, despite holding only 34.5% of combined voting power and just 5% of economic interest.

#### B. The Contested-Director Ceiling Is Hard-Capped at 49%

In a conventional dual-class structure, if Class A shareholders collectively acquire enough economic interest to overcome the 10:1 premium in combined voting, they can theoretically elect *all* directors.

Under SpaceX's charter, **Class A shareholders can never elect more than 49% of directors** (rounded down), regardless of how many shares they hold. With an 8-member board, that means at most 3 seats. The remaining 5 are structurally reserved for Class B.

This is not a practical disadvantage that can be overcome with capital—it is a **charter-level structural constraint** that persists until the last Class B share converts.

#### C. The 10:1 Premium Operates Only Within a Constrained Arena

In a conventional structure, the 10:1 premium operates across 100% of board seats and 100% of general matters. In SpaceX's structure, the 10:1 premium only determines outcomes for:
- The ~49% of board seats elected by combined vote (3 of 8 initially)
- All general shareholder matters (mergers, charter amendments not affecting Class B, etc.)

For the ~51% of board seats, the 10:1 premium is irrelevant—those seats are determined solely by the separate class vote.

---

### III. Specific Implication for a Class A Shareholder's Ability to Influence Board Composition

Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a **majority of economic interest** (say, 60%, 70%, or even 95% of shares outstanding), their ability to influence board composition is constrained in the following ways:

**1. Permanent exclusion from 51% of board seats:**
Class A shareholders have **zero** ability to nominate, elect, vote for, or remove Class B Directors. With the initial 8-member board, that means 5 of 8 seats (62.5%) are entirely beyond Class A's reach. This right is not a function of share ownership—it is a structural charter entitlement reserved exclusively for Class B holders.

**2. Severely constrained influence over the remaining ~49% of seats:**
For Common Stock Director seats (3 of 8 initially), Class A and Class B vote together. But Class B shares carry 10 votes each. Even if Class A shareholders held, hypothetically, 91% of shares (vs. 9% Class B), Class B's combined voting power would be:
- (9 × 10) / (9 × 10 + 91 × 1) = 90/181 = **49.7%**

Class A, despite holding 91% of economic interest, would hold barely 50.3% of combined voting power—enough for a razor-thin majority on just 3 seats. At any Class B economic interest above ~9.1%, the 10:1 premium alone gives Class B majority combined voting power, meaning Class B controls *all* seats.

**3. In practice, with disclosed ownership levels, Class A has effectively no influence:**
Pre-offering, Elon Musk held 5,569,053,075 Class B shares (carrying ~55.7 billion votes) and 849,494,440 Class A shares, representing ~88.9% of combined voting power. At these levels, Class B holders control both the separate-class vote (5 seats) and the combined vote (3 seats), giving them control of the **entire** board through both mechanisms simultaneously.

**4. No cumulative or proxy access relief:**
The filing does not disclose cumulative voting, proxy access, or any other mechanism that would allow minority Class A shareholders to concentrate votes on a subset of candidates. For the 3 Common Stock Director seats, Class B's vote weight in the combined election will typically determine the outcome.

---

### IV. Summary Comparison Table

| Dimension | Conventional 10:1 Dual-Class | SpaceX Dual-Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Director election method** | All directors elected by combined vote with 10:1 premium | 51% by Class B separate class vote; ~49% by combined vote |
| **Controlling class needs to hold** | Enough shares so that (shares × 10) > 50% of total voting power | Any non-zero number of Class B shares for 51% of board; voting-power majority for remaining seats |
| **Can economic majority by Class A overcome control?** | Yes, if Class A's share count overwhelms the 10:1 premium | **No**—51% of board is structurally locked for Class B regardless of economics |
| **Maximum board seats Class A can influence** | 100% (if they achieve voting-power majority) | ~49% (hard ceiling) |
| **Vulnerability to dilution** | Mathematically erodable | Class B Director seats are dilution-proof |
| **Persistence** | Ends when 10:1 premium is insufficient | Ends only when the last Class B share converts |

### V. Bottom Line

SpaceX's dual-mechanism structure is **strictly more protective of founder control** than a conventional 10:1 dual-class arrangement. The conventional structure relies solely on arithmetic—a premium that can, in theory, be diluted away. SpaceX's structure layers a **non-arithmetic, structural guarantee** (the separate class vote for 51% of directors) on top of the arithmetic premium. The practical implication is that a Class A shareholder—even one representing a consortium holding a majority of economic interest—cannot influence the election of a majority of the board and faces prohibitive disadvantages in contesting even the minority of seats technically open to combined voting. The Class B separate-class veto is absolute and binary: it exists or it does not, and as long as any Class B shares remain outstanding, it does.

---

**Sources:**
1.
